headline: Indigenous Women in India Turn Beekeeping Into Economic Independence
meta_description: UNESCO's Women for Bees project is helping 50 Indigenous women in southern India earn reliable income through native beekeeping, replacing risky traditional ho…
summary_short: 50 Indigenous women in India's Western Ghats are gaining financial independence through a UNESCO beekeeping initiative that uses native Asian honey bees.
body:
Fifty Indigenous women from Tamil Nadu and Kerala are experiencing newfound economic independence through a beekeeping initiative launched by UNESCO in partnership with the Keystone Foundation. The Women for Bees project, which began in April 2025, provides training and equipment to women from the Irula, Kurumba, and Kattunayaka communities to rear native Asian honey bees—a safer, more predictable alternative to the risky traditional wild honey hunting their ancestors practiced. The shift matters: while these communities historically possessed deep honey-hunting skills, forest protection laws now restrict the practice. One participant, Neelavathi from Tamil Nadu, has already used her earnings to support her family and purchase household goods. "Since I am the beekeeper at home, my family lets me keep the income for my own needs," she told Mongabay-India, capturing the quiet power of financial autonomy.

The choice of native Asian honey bee (Apis cerana indica) over the commercially dominant European species reflects both ecological wisdom and practical advantage. Research shows that Asian honey bee colonies are more resilient against local diseases and parasites, making them better suited to the Western Ghats ecosystem. Early results are striking: participants report good honey yields, with some women commanding premium prices for their product. The ecological benefits extend beyond the hive—studies suggest that Asian honey bee pollination improves yields of crops like bitter gourd, and some beekeepers have observed increased flowering and higher yields in neighboring fruit farms.

What distinguishes this project is its human retention and community impact. With a 100% retention rate among participants, the initiative has created more than economic opportunity—it has woven beekeeping into the social fabric. Lakshmi Babu, a participant from Kerala's Nilambur Elephant Reserve, noted that the project brought neighbors together to share knowledge and support one another. UNESCO organizers, encouraged by this success, plan to expand the model to other biosphere reserves.
quick_facts: 50 Indigenous women from Tamil Nadu and Kerala launched beekeeping in April 2025
Native Asian honey bees resist local diseases better than European varieties
Participant Neelavathi gained first financial independence through honey earnings
100% retention rate; project expanding to other biosphere reserves
